topic: Things that are Disliked in the Prayer (Makruhat)
title: Cracking or Interlacing the Fingers
short_rule: It is disliked to crack the knuckles or interlace the fingers during the prayer.
details: Both the Hanafi and Maliki schools explicitly classify cracking the knuckles or interlacing the fingers during the ritual prayer as a disliked action (makruh). In the Hanafi school, cracking the knuckles during prayer without any valid need is specifically considered prohibitively disliked (makruh tahriman).
actionable_steps: Avoid cracking your knuckles or interlacing your fingers while praying.
Keep your hands and fingers still and in the prescribed positions.
quran_hadith_evidence: Based on the general prohibition of fidgeting ('abath) and actions that detract from the reverence and stillness required in prayer.
spiritual_wisdom: Physical stillness reflects internal tranquility (Khushu'). Fidgeting with the hands distracts the mind from the divine presence and negates the humility of the worshipper.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn 'Ashir

topic: Things that are Disliked in the Prayer (Makruhat)
title: Placing Hands on the Hips or Waist
short_rule: It is disliked to pray with the hands placed on the hips or waist.
details: The Hanafi and Maliki schools agree that it is a disliked act to place one's hands on the hips or waist (with elbows pointing outward to the side) during the prayer.
actionable_steps: Keep your hands in the prescribed positions (folded or resting at the sides) during the standing postures.
Avoid resting your hands on your hips.
quran_hadith_evidence: Derived from prophetic traditions prohibiting 'ikhtisar' (praying with hands on hips), as it resembles the posture of arrogant people, those in rest, or the people of Hellfire.
spiritual_wisdom: Prayer is a state of absolute humility and submission; postures associated with arrogance, defiance, or casual resting contradict the profound servility required before Allah.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn 'Ashir

topic: Things that are Disliked in the Prayer (Makruhat)
title: Turning the Head or Neck
short_rule: Turning the head or neck to look to the side during prayer is disliked.
details: While turning the torso entirely away from the Qibla invalidates the prayer, merely turning the head or neck to look to the side is classified as a disliked act (makruh) by both the Hanafi and Maliki schools.
actionable_steps: Keep your gaze fixed on the prescribed locations, such as the place of prostration.
Avoid turning your neck or head to look around.
quran_hadith_evidence: The Prophet described looking around in prayer as 'a theft that Satan steals from the prayer of a servant.'
spiritual_wisdom: Looking around breaks the focus of the worshipper, symbolizing a heart that is turning away from Allah toward worldly distractions.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn 'Ashir

topic: Things that are Disliked in the Prayer (Makruhat)
title: Closing the Eyes During Prayer
short_rule: It is disliked to intentionally close one's eyes during the prayer.
details: Both the Hanafi and Maliki texts list closing the eyes as a disliked act during the ritual prayer. The Sunnah is to keep the eyes open and appropriately focused, unless closing them is necessary to achieve concentration.
actionable_steps: Keep your eyes open during the prayer.
Direct your gaze to the place of prostration to maintain concentration without needing to close your eyes.
quran_hadith_evidence: Based on the Prophet's established practice of keeping his eyes open and directing his gaze to specific points (like the place of prostration) during the prayer.
spiritual_wisdom: Keeping the eyes open and fixed demonstrates presence, alertness, and the proper etiquette (adab) of a servant standing attentively before their Master.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn 'Ashir

topic: Things that are Disliked in the Prayer (Makruhat)
title: Reciting Quran While Bowing or Prostrating
short_rule: It is disliked to recite verses of the Quran while in the bowing or prostrating postures.
details: The Maliki school explicitly notes that reciting the Quran while bowing (ruku') or prostrating (sujud) is a disliked act. The proper remembrance for these postures consists of glorification (tasbih) and supplication (du'a').
actionable_steps: Restrict Quranic recitation to the standing postures of the prayer.
Glorify Allah during bowing and make earnest supplications during prostration.
quran_hadith_evidence: The Prophet explicitly forbade reciting the Quran while bowing or prostrating, commanding instead that the Lord be glorified in ruku' and earnestly supplicated in sujud.
spiritual_wisdom: Different postures are designed for different types of remembrance. The majesty and authority of the Quran are reserved for the dignified standing posture, while bowing and prostrating are positions of humility designed for glorification and begging.
author: Ibn 'Ashir

topic: Things that are Disliked in the Prayer (Makruhat)
title: Reading Non-Quranic Text in Prayer
short_rule: Looking at and mentally reading a non-Quranic scripture during prayer is disliked.
details: According to the Hanbali and Shafi'i schools, if a worshipper looks at a written text or scripture that is not the Quran and reads or repeats it within their mind, the prayer itself is not invalidated (even if done for a long time). However, it is strictly classified as a disliked act.
actionable_steps: Avoid reading from any papers, books, or signs containing worldly or non-Quranic text while praying.
Keep your focus entirely on the prayer and its prescribed recitations.
quran_hadith_evidence: Based on the necessity of maintaining Khushu' (reverence) and the prohibition of engaging the mind in worldly discourse during the sacred state of worship.
spiritual_wisdom: The mind, like the body, must be oriented away from the mundane world and exclusively directed toward the divine conversation.
author: Ibn Qudamah
